dimensions.dimension_name: Emotional vs Analytical Ad Processing Style
dimensions.what_it_captures: Whether the person processes advertising primarily through emotional/visceral reactions (mood, music, visuals, storytelling) or through analytical evaluation (claims, comparisons, evidence). This is distinct from what content they prefer (Q59 covers content type preference) — this is about the cognitive mode they default to when an ad interrupts their feed. Do they feel first and think later, or scrutinize first and feel later?
dimensions.concept_test_impact: An analytical processor would dissect a product concept's ingredient list, compare claims against competitors, and flag inconsistencies before forming an opinion. An emotional processor would react to the overall 'vibe' of the concept — does it feel premium, does it feel like 'me' — before engaging with specifics. The same concept with a strong emotional narrative but weak ingredient backing would convert one and repel the other.
dimensions.ad_test_impact: A cinematic, mood-driven video ad with ambient music and lifestyle imagery would deeply engage an emotional processor but frustrate an analytical one who wants to see data or claims upfront. Conversely, an ad leading with '87% saw reduced oiliness in 2 weeks' would hook the analytical processor but feel cold and clinical to the emotional one.
dimensions.what_we_can_infer: His ranking of expert opinion and ingredients first (Q11), preference for ingredient breakdowns (Q59), and use of ChatGPT for research (Q34) suggest analytical tendencies. However, he also values 'aesthetic content' (Q59), was drawn to Bath & Body Works by the 'aesthetic mall store experience' (Q23), and his purchase of Plum was partly driven by heavy visual social media campaigns (Q58). This tension makes his default processing mode genuinely uncertain — he may switch modes contextually, but we don't know his baseline.
dimensions.example_variation: Twin A (emotional processor) sees a body wash ad featuring a confident young man walking through Mumbai monsoon rain, cinematic shots, no voiceover, just a tagline — feels inspired, saves the ad, looks up the brand. Twin B (analytical processor) sees the same ad, finds it visually nice but empty, scrolls past because no ingredient or efficacy information was presented, never engages further.

dimensions.dimension_name: Social Identity Signaling Through Product Choices
dimensions.what_it_captures: The degree to which this person uses personal care products as social identity markers — whether the brand they use, display in their bathroom, or mention in conversation is part of how they construct and project their identity to peers. This goes beyond caring about how they smell (Q8) to whether the brand itself carries social currency in their peer group.
dimensions.concept_test_impact: A person high on identity signaling would respond strongly to a concept positioned as a 'status' or 'insider knowledge' brand — limited drops, distinctive packaging visible in a shower selfie, brand story that signals taste. A person low on this dimension would evaluate the same concept purely on functional merit and find status-oriented positioning irrelevant or off-putting.
dimensions.ad_test_impact: An ad showing a product prominently placed in a stylish apartment bathroom, or a peer-group scene where someone compliments the user's scent and asks 'what do you use?', would strongly resonate with a high-signaling person. A low-signaling person would find this superficial and prefer an ad focused on skin outcomes or ingredient science.
dimensions.what_we_can_infer: He was drawn to Bath & Body Works partly for the premium mall experience (Q23), suggesting some aspirational signaling. But he primarily buys from Blinkit for convenience (Q25), uses Dettol daily (Q28), and shows low brand loyalty (Q17) — none of which suggest strong identity attachment to brands. His family context (sister and mother are particular about products, Q32) hints at a household where product choices are noticed, but his own level of social signaling through products remains unclear.
dimensions.example_variation: Twin A (high signaling) sees a concept for a premium D2C body wash with Instagram-worthy packaging and a 'curated for those who know' positioning — immediately imagines it in their bathroom, considers how friends would perceive it, rates high purchase intent. Twin B (low signaling) sees the same concept, ignores the positioning, checks if the ingredients justify the price, and rates it average because the formulation isn't differentiated enough.

dimensions.dimension_name: Tolerance for Aspirational vs Authentic Representation
dimensions.what_it_captures: Whether the person responds better to advertising that shows an aspirational, idealized version of life (perfect skin, luxury settings, elevated lifestyle) or authentic, relatable representation (real skin textures, everyday Indian settings, imperfect but genuine scenarios). This is not about influencer trust (Q55-Q57) but about the visual and narrative register that triggers engagement vs. skepticism.
dimensions.concept_test_impact: When shown a product concept with aspirational imagery (model with flawless skin, premium bathroom setting, international aesthetic), a person who prefers aspirational framing would perceive higher quality and desirability. A person who prefers authenticity would find it disconnected from their reality and question whether the product actually delivers. The same concept with 'real user' photography would reverse these reactions.
dimensions.ad_test_impact: A glossy, highly produced ad with a Bollywood-adjacent aesthetic would excite an aspirational-leaning viewer but trigger skepticism in an authenticity-leaning one. An ad showing a regular guy in a typical Mumbai apartment bathroom, with natural lighting and genuine skin texture, would feel trustworthy to the latter but 'cheap' or 'unambitious' to the former.
dimensions.what_we_can_infer: He values relatability in influencers — 'similar location context and income level' (Q55) — which suggests authenticity preference. But he was drawn to Bath & Body Works' premium aesthetic (Q23), prefers 'aesthetic content' (Q59), and wants 'minimalist packaging' (Q41/Q49), which could indicate aspirational taste. The tension between his stated preference for relatable influencers and his attraction to premium aesthetics makes this dimension genuinely ambiguous.
dimensions.example_variation: Twin A (aspirational-tolerant) sees a body wash ad shot like a luxury fragrance commercial — slow-motion water, chiseled model, dramatic lighting — and perceives the product as high-quality, worth exploring. Twin B (authenticity-seeking) sees the same ad and immediately categorizes it as 'all marketing, no substance,' feels it's trying too hard, and scrolls past.

dimensions.dimension_name: Decision Urgency and Deliberation Window Length
dimensions.what_it_captures: The temporal gap between first exposure to a product concept and the point at which the person commits to a purchase decision — and how much active deliberation occurs in that window. Some people see a compelling concept and add to cart within minutes; others bookmark, research over days/weeks, wait for a trigger moment. This is distinct from purchase channel (Q20/Q25) and discovery source (Q54) — it's about the internal decision timeline.
dimensions.concept_test_impact: A short-deliberation person would respond to concept tests with more decisive purchase intent scores — they'd quickly decide 'yes I'd buy' or 'no.' A long-deliberation person would rate concepts more moderately, hedging with 'I'd look into it' or 'maybe,' even for concepts they'd eventually purchase. This affects how we interpret their concept test scores — a '3/5' from a long-deliberator might actually convert better than a '4/5' from a short-deliberator who forgets by tomorrow.
dimensions.ad_test_impact: Ads with urgency cues (limited edition, 'selling fast,' time-bound offers) would accelerate a short-deliberation person but feel manipulative to a long-deliberation person. Ads designed to plant a seed (brand awareness, no CTA, just memorable imagery) would work well for long-deliberators who need multiple touchpoints but would feel incomplete to short-deliberators who want to act now.
dimensions.what_we_can_infer: The Plum purchase (Q58) shows a multi-touchpoint journey — saw on social media, then saw on Blinkit, then researched, then bought — suggesting longer deliberation. But his Blinkit usage (Q25) shows impulsive, need-based purchasing ('realizes product is empty right before bathing'). He may have two distinct modes: deliberate for new brand trials and impulsive for replenishment. But we don't know which mode would activate during a concept test or ad exposure, making his response patterns unpredictable.
dimensions.example_variation: Twin A (short deliberation) sees a compelling body wash concept in an Instagram ad, clicks through, reads the product page for 30 seconds, adds to cart on Blinkit if available. Twin B (long deliberation) sees the same ad, screenshots it, checks the brand's Instagram page over the next few days, reads reviews on Nykaa, waits until their current body wash runs out, then finally purchases — but rates the ad identically to Twin A in an immediate survey.

dimensions.dimension_name: Masculinity Framing Sensitivity in Personal Care
dimensions.what_it_captures: How the person responds to gendered positioning in body wash marketing — whether they prefer explicitly masculine framing ('for men,' rugged imagery, deep scents), gender-neutral/unisex positioning, or are even open to traditionally feminine-coded aesthetics (floral, soft, pastel). This goes beyond fragrance preference (Q35-Q37) to encompass the entire gendered semiotics of product presentation — colors, language, imagery, brand voice, and whether gendered cues feel affirming or limiting.
dimensions.concept_test_impact: A concept positioned with strong masculine codes (dark packaging, 'built for men,' charcoal/wood imagery) would appeal to someone who wants their body wash to reinforce masculine identity, but would feel reductive to someone who sees personal care as gender-neutral. Conversely, a unisex concept with soft, pastel, ingredient-forward branding would attract the gender-neutral person but might feel 'not for me' to the masculinity-affirming one — even if the formulation is identical.
dimensions.ad_test_impact: An ad featuring a hyper-masculine narrative (gym, sports, 'man up' energy) would resonate with one end of this spectrum but alienate the other. An ad with a softer, self-care-oriented narrative (relaxation, skin health, 'treat yourself') would reverse these reactions. The same product could succeed or fail based entirely on which gendered register the ad adopts.
dimensions.what_we_can_infer: He wants a 'masculine aftershave-like fragrance' (Q49) and follows male actors/beauty influencers (Q55), suggesting some alignment with masculine framing. He rated male-targeted brands like The Man Company and Beardo low (Q48: 4 and 2), but rated gender-neutral brands like Plum and Minimalist highest (Q48: 5). He uses Dettol (gender-neutral) and previously used Dove and Pears (often feminine-coded). This creates genuine ambiguity — he articulates masculine preferences in his ideal product but his actual behavior and brand ratings don't consistently favor masculine-positioned brands.
dimensions.example_variation: Twin A (masculinity-affirming) sees a body wash concept with clean, dark packaging, tagline 'Engineered for Men's Skin,' and a male model — feels the product understands him, rates high purchase intent. Twin B (gender-neutral) sees the same concept and finds the 'for men' positioning unnecessary and gimmicky, prefers the Minimalist-style unisex concept shown next, rates the masculine one lower despite identical ingredients.
